content.text: Archive tiering for AI data is the practice of automatically migrating AI-related data — training datasets, model artefacts, evaluation results, inference logs, agent memory snapshots, and vector store indexes — across a hierarchy of storage tiers based on the data's access frequency, business criticality, regulatory retention obligation, and cost profile. The goal is to serve frequently-accessed data from high-performance, higher-cost storage, while automatically moving infrequently-accessed data to progressively cheaper storage as it ages, without losing the ability to retrieve it when needed for audit, retraining, or compliance.
What It Is

Archive tiering defines four storage tiers — hot, warm, cold, and frozen — each characterised by a different trade-off between storage cost, retrieval latency, and minimum storage duration. Hot storage (NVMe SSD, in-memory cache) serves data that must be available within milliseconds: the active vector index, the online feature store, the inference cache. Warm storage (standard SSD or object storage) serves data accessed within the last 30 to 180 days at latencies of seconds: recent conversation logs, evaluation run results, recent model checkpoints. Cold storage (object storage with infrequent access tiers, such as AWS S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval) serves compliance-retention data accessed rarely at latencies of seconds to minutes: audit logs older than 90 days, training dataset snapshots for retired model versions. Frozen storage (deep archive, tape) serves data that must be retained for legal or regulatory reasons but is expected to be accessed only for rare audits at latencies of hours: original training corpora, long-term model artefacts for regulatory audit, raw data under legal hold.

The tiering policy defines the rules that govern when data transitions between tiers. Policies are expressed as a combination of age-based rules (move data to cold storage after 180 days), access-pattern rules (move data back to warm if it has been accessed more than five times in the last 30 days), and event-based rules (move data to frozen immediately if a legal hold is applied; delete data when the retention period expires and no hold is active). Lifecycle automation applies these policies continuously, without manual intervention, using the storage platform's native lifecycle management APIs.
Why It Matters
AI data volumes are enormous and growing:

A large language model training run may produce petabytes of data: the training corpus, intermediate checkpoints, optimizer states, evaluation results, and final artefacts. Storing everything on hot storage is cost-prohibitive. Without tiering, organisations either overspend on storage or manually delete data that they later need.
Regulatory retention requirements are mandatory:

Financial services regulators require that model inputs, outputs, and supporting data be retained for 5 to 7 years. Healthcare records must be retained for 10 years or longer. Without cold and frozen tiers, meeting these retention requirements at acceptable cost is not feasible.
Inference costs scale with data volume:

RAG systems that retrieve from a single, undifferentiated vector index containing both recent active documents and archived documents from years ago incur unnecessary retrieval overhead. Tiering the vector index — active index on hot storage, historical index on warm storage — reduces retrieval cost and latency for the common case.
Model reproduction requires training data retention:

If a model's behaviour is disputed in a regulatory or legal proceeding, the organisation must be able to reproduce the model using the training data and code from the relevant period. This requires that training datasets from potentially several years ago be retrievable within a reasonable time — achievable from cold storage but not if the data was deleted.
Cost optimisation at scale:

S3 Standard costs approximately $23 per terabyte-month; S3 Glacier Deep Archive costs approximately $1 per terabyte-month. For a petabyte of compliance-retention data, the difference is $22,000 per month — $264,000 per year — per petabyte. At AI data volumes, tiering is a significant budget lever.
Benefits
Cost reduction:

Moving data from hot to cold storage as it ages reduces storage costs by up to 95% for the coldest tier, enabling compliance retention at a fraction of the cost of keeping all data on standard storage.
Performance isolation:

Tiering ensures that large volumes of historical data do not degrade the performance of hot-tier data stores. The active vector index remains fast because it does not contain documents from three years ago.
Automated compliance:

Lifecycle policies enforce retention periods without manual monitoring or deletion jobs. When a retention period expires, the lifecycle policy deletes the data automatically — preventing over-retention, which is itself a compliance risk under data minimisation principles.
Disaster recovery alignment:
Tiering policies can be aligned with backup and DR strategies, ensuring that frozen-tier data is replicated to a geographically separate region for business continuity.
Operational simplicity:

Once tiering policies are configured, the storage platform manages transitions automatically. The data engineering team does not need to write and maintain custom deletion or migration scripts for each data class.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Retrieval latency from cold and frozen tiers:

Data in Glacier Instant Retrieval is available in milliseconds; Glacier Flexible Retrieval takes 3 to 5 hours; Glacier Deep Archive takes 12 to 48 hours. For scenarios where archived training data must be accessed for a model reproduction exercise, multi-hour retrieval latency must be factored into the timeline.
Early deletion fees:

Cold and frozen storage tiers charge minimum storage durations (90 days for Glacier Instant Retrieval, 180 days for Glacier Deep Archive on AWS). Deleting data before the minimum duration still incurs the full minimum fee. Organisations that need flexibility to delete data early must account for these costs.
Retrieval cost at scale:

Retrieving data from cold or frozen storage incurs a per-GB retrieval fee. For large-scale retrieval — such as restoring a multi-terabyte training dataset from Glacier for retraining — the retrieval fee can be significant. The retrieval cost must be modelled as part of the total cost of ownership.
Access pattern prediction is imperfect:

Tiering policies based on age assumptions may be wrong: a training dataset that has been idle for a year may suddenly be needed for a regulatory audit. Moving it back from cold storage is possible but takes time. Some organisations maintain a warm copy of compliance-critical data to hedge against unexpected access needs.
Vector index tiering requires re-embedding:

Moving a vector index from hot to warm or cold storage does not automatically handle the case where documents in the index are modified or new documents must be inserted while the old documents are archived. The indexing strategy must account for the tiered nature of the data.
How It Works

Archive tiering works through three mechanisms operating in concert: lifecycle policies (rules that transition objects between storage classes automatically based on age and other attributes), access pattern analysis (monitoring systems that detect when data is being accessed more or less frequently than the current tier is designed for and recommending tier adjustments), and event-driven transitions (explicit tier changes triggered by business events such as a model being retired, a legal hold being applied, or a retention period expiring).

Lifecycle policies are the primary mechanism for cost optimisation. On AWS S3, a lifecycle policy is a set of rules applied to objects matching a prefix or tag. Each rule specifies a source storage class, a target storage class, and a transition trigger (age in days, or a specific date). For example: objects with the tag data_class=training_dataset move to S3 Standard-IA after 90 days, to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 180 days, and are deleted after 2555 days (7 years). Equivalent features exist on Azure Blob Storage (lifecycle management policies) and Google Cloud Storage (lifecycle rules).

Access pattern analysis uses storage access logs to detect warm data that has been promoted back to frequent access — for example, a model checkpoint that was moved to warm storage but is being accessed daily because a production incident triggered a rollback to that version. The analysis compares actual access frequency against the tier's access frequency assumption and generates recommendations for tier adjustment. For S3, AWS S3 Intelligent-Tiering automates this analysis and transitions objects automatically between frequent and infrequent access classes without requiring explicit lifecycle rules.

Event-driven transitions are implemented using the storage platform's API triggered from the governance system. When the data governance platform records a legal hold being applied to a subject's data, it calls the storage API to add a retention lock to the corresponding objects, preventing their deletion by lifecycle policies until the hold is lifted. When a model is retired, the model registry triggers a lifecycle event that moves the model's training data and artefacts to cold storage and sets a new retention expiry based on the regulatory requirement.
Architecture and Design

attrs.alt: Archive Tiering for AI Data: four storage tiers (hot, warm, cold, frozen) with latency, cost, and TTL profiles and AI data classification by tier

content.text: The tiering architecture for AI data has three layers: the storage layer (the actual storage tiers and the objects they hold), the lifecycle management layer (the policies and automation that govern transitions), and the governance integration layer (the connection between the storage lifecycle system and the data governance platform).

The storage layer maps each AI data class to a default starting tier and a tiering trajectory. Active agent memory and the hot vector index start on hot storage and move to warm after 30 days of no access. Training datasets start on warm storage (they are batch data, not real-time) and move to cold after 180 days, then to frozen at the 2-year mark with a 7-year retention lock. Inference logs start on warm and move to cold after 90 days. Model artefacts for production models stay on warm; artefacts for retired models move to cold after 90 days and to frozen after 180 days.

The governance integration layer is the critical differentiator from a plain storage lifecycle configuration. It connects the storage lifecycle system to the data governance platform so that: data classification tags flow from the governance metadata store to the storage objects at write time, enabling tier-appropriate lifecycle rules to apply automatically; legal holds applied in the governance platform translate to Object Lock configurations on the storage tier; retention period changes approved in the governance platform propagate to lifecycle rule updates; and erasure requests from the privacy management system are validated against legal hold status before deletion is permitted.
When to Use It
When AI data volumes exceed 10 TB and storage costs are a significant operational expense.
When regulatory retention requirements mandate keeping AI training data, model artefacts, or inference logs for 5 years or longer.
When RAG systems suffer from retrieval latency caused by large historical document corpora in the same index as active content.
When the data engineering team is spending significant time managing manual deletion jobs or storage migration scripts.
When the organisation needs to demonstrate to a regulator that data is retained for the required period and deleted when the retention period expires.
When model artefacts for multiple retired model generations are accumulating and consuming expensive storage.
When compliance requirements mandate that data cannot be deleted (legal holds) and the organisation needs a technical mechanism to enforce retention locks.
How to Implement

Inventory all AI data classes (training data, model checkpoints, eval results, inference logs, agent memory, vector indexes) and define a tiering trajectory for each: starting tier, transition ages, and final retention or deletion.

Tag all AI data objects at write time with governance metadata: data_class, retention_years, data_classification, model_id or dataset_version. Tags are the mechanism by which lifecycle policies identify objects.
Configure lifecycle policies in the storage platform for each data_class tag value, implementing the tiering trajectory defined in step 1.
Integrate the governance platform with the storage API to propagate legal holds as Object Lock configurations and erasure requests as validated deletes.
Configure access logging on all storage tiers and set up a monitoring dashboard showing objects by tier, estimated cost per tier, and upcoming lifecycle transitions.
Run a cost model comparing current storage costs against projected costs with tiering to quantify the business case and validate the tiering trajectory assumptions.
Test the lifecycle policies with a non-production dataset: verify that objects transition to the expected tier at the expected age and that legal holds prevent deletion.

# Configure S3 lifecycle policy for AI data tiering
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

def configure_ai_data_lifecycle(bucket_name: str):
    """Configure S3 lifecycle rules for AI data tiering."""
    lifecycle_config = {
        'Rules': [
            {
                'ID': 'training-data-tiering',
                'Status': 'Enabled',
                'Filter': {'Tag': {'Key': 'data_class', 'Value': 'training_dataset'}},
                'Transitions': [
                    {'Days': 90,  'StorageClass': 'STANDARD_IA'},
                    {'Days': 180, 'StorageClass': 'GLACIER_IR'},   # Instant Retrieval
                    {'Days': 730, 'StorageClass': 'DEEP_ARCHIVE'},  # 2 years
                ],
                'Expiration': {'Days': 2555},  # 7 years, then auto-delete
            },
            {
                'ID': 'model-artifacts-tiering',
                'Status': 'Enabled',
                'Filter': {'Tag': {'Key': 'data_class', 'Value': 'model_artifact'}},
                'Transitions': [
                    {'Days': 180, 'StorageClass': 'STANDARD_IA'},
                    {'Days': 365, 'StorageClass': 'GLACIER_IR'},
                ],
                'Expiration': {'Days': 2555},  # 7 years
            },
            {
                'ID': 'inference-logs-tiering',
                'Status': 'Enabled',
                'Filter': {'Tag': {'Key': 'data_class', 'Value': 'inference_log'}},
                'Transitions': [
                    {'Days': 30,  'StorageClass': 'STANDARD_IA'},
                    {'Days': 90,  'StorageClass': 'GLACIER_IR'},
                    {'Days': 365, 'StorageClass': 'DEEP_ARCHIVE'},
                ],
                'Expiration': {'Days': 1825},  # 5 years
            },
            {
                'ID': 'agent-memory-tiering',
                'Status': 'Enabled',
                'Filter': {'Tag': {'Key': 'data_class', 'Value': 'agent_memory'}},
                'Transitions': [
                    {'Days': 30,  'StorageClass': 'STANDARD_IA'},
                    {'Days': 90,  'StorageClass': 'GLACIER_IR'},
                ],
                'Expiration': {'Days': 730},  # 2 years
            },
        ]
    }
    s3.put_bucket_lifecycle_configuration(
        Bucket=bucket_name,
        LifecycleConfiguration=lifecycle_config
    )
    print(f"Lifecycle policy configured for bucket: {bucket_name}")

configure_ai_data_lifecycle('my-ai-data-bucket')

# Apply retention lock (Object Lock) for legal holds
import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

def apply_legal_hold(bucket: str, prefix: str, hold_until: datetime):
    """Apply S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode to all objects under a prefix."""
    paginator = s3.get_paginator('list_objects_v2')
    pages = paginator.paginate(Bucket=bucket, Prefix=prefix)

    locked_count = 0
    for page in pages:
        for obj in page.get('Contents', []):
            s3.put_object_retention(
                Bucket=bucket,
                Key=obj['Key'],
                Retention={
                    'Mode': 'COMPLIANCE',   # COMPLIANCE: cannot be overridden by any user
                    'RetainUntilDate': hold_until
                }
            )
            locked_count += 1

    print(f"Applied COMPLIANCE lock to {locked_count} objects under s3://{bucket}/{prefix}")
    return locked_count

def lift_legal_hold(bucket: str, prefix: str):
    """
    Note: COMPLIANCE mode locks cannot be shortened.
    This method documents the governance decision; actual lock expiry is time-based.
    Only GOVERNANCE mode locks can be overridden with appropriate IAM permissions.
    """
    # Record the lift decision in the governance log (the lock itself expires at RetainUntilDate)
    print(f"Legal hold lift recorded for s3://{bucket}/{prefix}; lock expires at RetainUntilDate")

# Example: lock training data for an active litigation hold, until 2028-01-01
hold_until = datetime(2028, 1, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
apply_legal_hold('my-ai-data-bucket', 'training-data/customer-support-v2/', hold_until)

# Tag AI data objects with governance metadata at write time
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

def upload_training_dataset_with_tags(
    local_path: str,
    bucket: str,
    s3_key: str,
    dataset_id: str,
    version: str,
    retention_years: int = 7,
):
    """Upload a training dataset file with governance tags for lifecycle policy routing."""
    tags = {
        'data_class': 'training_dataset',
        'dataset_id': dataset_id,
        'dataset_version': version,
        'data_classification': 'internal',
        'retention_years': str(retention_years),
        'uploaded_at': '2026-07-19',
    }

    tag_string = '&'.join(f"{k}={v}" for k, v in tags.items())

    with open(local_path, 'rb') as f:
        s3.upload_fileobj(
            f, bucket, s3_key,
            ExtraArgs={'Tagging': tag_string}
        )

    print(f"Uploaded {local_path} to s3://{bucket}/{s3_key} with governance tags")
    return f"s3://{bucket}/{s3_key}"
Examples

# Azure Blob Storage lifecycle management policy for AI data
rules:
  - name: training-data-tiering
    enabled: true
    type: Lifecycle
    definition:
      filters:
        blobTypes: [blockBlob]
        blobIndexMatch:
          - name: data_class
            op: ==
            value: training_dataset
      actions:
        baseBlob:
          tierToCool:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 90
          tierToArchive:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 180
          delete:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 2555  # 7 years

  - name: model-artifacts-tiering
    enabled: true
    type: Lifecycle
    definition:
      filters:
        blobTypes: [blockBlob]
        blobIndexMatch:
          - name: data_class
            op: ==
            value: model_artifact
      actions:
        baseBlob:
          tierToCool:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 180
          tierToArchive:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 365
          delete:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 2555

  - name: inference-logs-tiering
    enabled: true
    type: Lifecycle
    definition:
      filters:
        blobTypes: [blockBlob]
        blobIndexMatch:
          - name: data_class
            op: ==
            value: inference_log
      actions:
        baseBlob:
          tierToCool:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 30
          tierToArchive:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 90
          delete:
            daysAfterModificationGreaterThan: 1825  # 5 years
Real-World Example

A financial services firm operates a regulatory compliance agent that processes trade communications and flags potential compliance violations. The firm is subject to MiFID II, which requires that records of trade communications be retained for 5 years. The firm also runs model training on these communications quarterly and retains model artefacts for 7 years for potential regulatory audit.

Before implementing archive tiering, the firm stored all data on S3 Standard. The accumulated storage bill was $180,000 per month for 8 petabytes of total AI data, of which 85% was compliance-retention data accessed fewer than once per year. Implementing a tiering policy moved the compliance-retention data to S3 Glacier Deep Archive, reducing the monthly storage bill to $22,000 — a $158,000 monthly saving ($1.9 million annually).

The implementation also resolved a performance problem: the compliance agent's vector index had grown to include documents from 5 years of trade communications, causing retrieval latency to increase from 150ms to 2.8 seconds as the index grew. By tiering the vector index — maintaining only the most recent 90 days of documents in the hot vector index and moving older documents to a cold vector index that is queried only when explicitly requested — the retrieval latency for the common case (recent communications) returned to 150ms. The cold index is queried only for historical investigations, which can tolerate higher latency.

The governance integration layer connects the legal team's litigation hold management tool to S3 Object Lock. When the legal team applies a hold to a specific counterparty's communications (because of an ongoing regulatory investigation), the governance platform queries the inference log and communication metadata stores for all records associated with that counterparty and applies COMPLIANCE-mode Object Lock to those objects, preventing their deletion by the lifecycle policy until the hold is lifted.
Common Mistakes
Not tagging objects at write time:

Lifecycle policies that rely on object tags cannot be applied retrospectively if the tags were not applied when the object was created. All AI data write paths must include governance metadata tagging as a required step, not an optional afterthought.
Confusing GOVERNANCE mode with COMPLIANCE mode Object Lock:

GOVERNANCE mode Object Lock can be overridden by users with the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission; COMPLIANCE mode cannot be overridden by any user, including the root account. For legal holds and regulatory retention, use COMPLIANCE mode.
Not accounting for retrieval costs in the cost model:

Organisations that model storage savings without modelling retrieval costs may be surprised by significant retrieval fees when a regulatory audit requires restoring large volumes from deep archive. Model both storage and retrieval costs for each tier.
Tiering vector indexes without re-indexing:

Moving a vector index file to cold storage makes it inaccessible for real-time similarity search. Tiering vector data requires splitting the index by time period (or another dimension) and maintaining the active period in hot storage while archiving older periods.
Setting retention periods without legal review:

Retention periods must be set based on legal advice for each data type and jurisdiction, not engineering convenience. Setting 5-year retention when the regulation requires 7 years, or setting 10-year retention when the data minimisation principle requires deletion at 5 years, both create compliance risk.
Not testing lifecycle transitions:

Lifecycle policies that have never been validated in a non-production environment may have unintended effects — transitioning the wrong objects, missing objects due to tag inconsistencies, or failing to delete data when the expiry rule triggers. Test each rule with synthetic data before applying it to production.
Best Practices
Define retention requirements before designing the tiering policy:
Tiering policy design depends on retention requirements. Get legal sign-off on retention periods for each data class before configuring lifecycle rules.
Apply S3 Intelligent-Tiering for unpredictable access patterns:

For AI data with unpredictable access patterns (e.g., training datasets that are accessed rarely but suddenly needed for a retraining run), S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves data between frequent and infrequent access classes based on actual access patterns, without per-object monitoring fees for objects over 128 KB.
Use cost allocation tags to track spending by data class:

Tag all AI data objects with a cost_center or data_class tag and enable cost allocation in the storage platform's billing console. This makes the storage cost of each AI data class visible and attributable.
Implement a pre-deletion notification:

Configure a notification (SNS topic, EventBridge rule) that fires 30 days before an object's lifecycle expiry. The notification gives the data owner a window to review the upcoming deletion and apply a retention extension if necessary.
Maintain a warm copy of compliance-critical data:

For data that a regulator may request with little notice (e.g., model artefacts for a currently active model), consider maintaining a warm copy alongside the cold archive to avoid retrieval latency delays in an audit scenario.
Audit lifecycle policy effectiveness quarterly:

Review the distribution of objects across tiers quarterly to confirm that the lifecycle policy is producing the expected tier distribution and storage cost profile. Adjust transition ages if the actual access pattern differs from the design assumption.
Performance and Scalability

Lifecycle policy transitions are applied by the storage platform asynchronously. On AWS S3, the transition SLA is that objects eligible for transition on day N are transitioned within a few days. This means lifecycle policy transitions are not instantaneous — an object that becomes eligible for cold storage transition on a given day may remain on standard storage for up to a few days longer. For cost modelling, this delay is negligible; for SLA commitments that require data to be in cold storage by a specific date, the transition delay must be accounted for.

For very large AI data environments (hundreds of petabytes), the volume of lifecycle transition operations can be significant. AWS charges for lifecycle transition operations at approximately $0.01 per 1,000 objects transitioned. For an environment with one billion objects, transitioning 10% of them in a single day would cost approximately $1,000 in transition fees — not negligible but typically small relative to the storage savings.

Vector index tiering at scale requires a different approach from file-level tiering. The vector index is typically a structured binary file (FAISS index, Annoy index) or a set of structured records in a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate). Tiering a vector database requires: partitioning the index by time period, directing queries to the hot partition for recent data and the warm/cold partitions for historical data, and using a query routing layer that merges results from multiple partitions when a historical query is made. This is more complex than file-level tiering but essential for maintaining performance at scale.
Tooling and Ecosystem

All major cloud storage platforms provide native lifecycle management: AWS S3 lifecycle policies with S3 Intelligent-Tiering and Object Lock; Azure Blob Storage lifecycle management with immutable blob storage; Google Cloud Storage lifecycle rules with retention policies. For on-premises environments, MinIO provides S3-compatible object storage with lifecycle management. Data tiering tools that work across cloud and on-premises environments include Komprise and Atempo, which add governance-aware tiering on top of storage platforms. For vector index tiering specifically, Weaviate supports multi-tenancy with per-tenant tier assignment; Pinecone supports serverless indexes that automatically scale storage based on data size.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Cost Profile
Compliance Fit
Limitations
All data on standard storage
Very high ($$$$$)
Easy to manage; no retrieval delays
Prohibitively expensive at AI data volumes; over-retention risk
Manual deletion scripts
Lower cost but labor-intensive
Only as good as script coverage
Error-prone; may delete data under legal hold; not auditable
Fixed retention on standard storage + annual delete
High; reduces over time
Meets retention if periods are correct
No cost optimisation within retention period; no hold support
Archive tiering with lifecycle policies (this topic)
Optimised (10-20x cost reduction)
Full hold support, automated deletion, retention locks
Setup complexity; retrieval latency from cold/frozen; transition fees
Security and Reliability

Data at rest in cold and frozen storage must be encrypted with a key management policy consistent with the data's classification. For data classified as confidential or above, use customer-managed keys (AWS KMS CMK, Azure Key Vault) so that the organisation retains control of the encryption keys. If a data classification event requires that a large volume of archived data be made permanently inaccessible (for example, a serious data breach), revoking the encryption key renders all data encrypted with that key permanently unrecoverable — this is sometimes called cryptographic erasure and is accepted as equivalent to physical deletion under GDPR for encrypted data where the key is destroyed.

Reliability of archived data depends on the storage platform's durability guarantees. AWS S3 offers 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability across all storage classes, including Glacier Deep Archive. However, durability does not protect against accidental or malicious deletion. Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode provides the primary protection against accidental deletion. Additionally, critical AI artefacts (training datasets, model checkpoints for active production models) should be replicated to a second region using S3 Cross-Region Replication with replication rules that include Glacier objects, ensuring geo-redundancy for compliance-critical data.
Mental Model

Think of archive tiering as the filing system in a law firm. Current case files (hot) sit on the lawyer's desk, within arm's reach and accessed multiple times per day. Cases from the past year (warm) are in the filing room down the hall — accessible within minutes. Cases from 5 to 10 years ago (cold) are in an off-site document storage facility — retrievable within a day if needed. Cases from more than 10 years ago (frozen) are in a long-term archive warehouse — retrievable but it takes two or three days and the files have to be physically retrieved and transported. And some cases are under a legal hold — they cannot be shredded regardless of how old they are, because an ongoing legal matter requires their preservation. The tiering policy is the office manager who decides which drawer or facility each file belongs in, moves files between locations as they age, and ensures that files under a hold are never accidentally shredded.
Key Takeaways

Archive tiering moves AI data across hot, warm, cold, and frozen storage tiers automatically, based on access frequency and business events, optimising storage cost by up to 95% for compliance-retention data.
All AI data objects must be tagged with governance metadata (data_class, retention_years, legal_hold_status) at write time — retroactive tagging is expensive and error-prone.
Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode is the technical mechanism for legal holds; GOVERNANCE mode can be overridden and is insufficient for legal hold requirements.
Vector index tiering requires partitioning the index by time period and a query routing layer — it cannot be achieved by simply moving the index file to cold storage.
Retrieval costs from cold and frozen tiers must be modelled alongside storage costs; the total cost of ownership may differ significantly from the storage cost alone.
Lifecycle policy transitions are asynchronous; plan for a few days of delay between eligibility and actual transition when designing time-sensitive compliance controls.
